items.passage: In a forest, every plant and animal is part of a food web. Green leaves are eaten by deer and rabbits, which in turn feed wolves and large cats. When these animals die, small bugs and fungi break down the bodies and return the parts to the soil. This rich soil helps new trees grow tall again.

Wetlands are low, wet places where land and water meet for most of the year. They act like sponges that soak up heavy rain and slowly release it during dry months. Many birds, fish, and frogs live in these areas, and rare plants grow along the muddy banks. However, drained wetlands lose this power and turn into hard ground.

Sleep is the time when the brain sorts and stores what we learn each day. During deep stages, weak memories are made stronger and useless details slip away. People who rest for fewer than six hours often feel slow and find it hard to focus on simple tasks. Good sleep also helps to lift mood and lower daily stress.

The ancient Maya built great stone cities deep in the jungles of Central America. They grew corn, beans, and squash on land that was cleared from the thick forest. Tall pyramids were raised in the heart of each city, and priests used them to watch the stars and track the seasons. Their written signs still puzzle many modern readers.

Most large caves form when slightly acid rain water slowly eats away soft limestone rock. The water sinks through small cracks and, over thousands of years, opens wide rooms and long tunnels under the ground. Drops that fall from the ceiling leave behind thin layers of stone, which build up into pointed shapes called stalactites and stalagmites.

Satellites are machines that circle the Earth and send useful signals back to the ground. Some are placed in low orbits to take sharp photos of weather, crops, and storms. Others sit much higher up, where they help phones and ships find their exact place on the planet. Although they look small, these tools shape modern life in many quiet ways.
